text: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor: Idk if it's underrated, but I rather like the 6th floor of the library. It's the quiet floor and very few people go up there (granted, that was my experience last semester, so we were still partly online)
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor

text: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor: o we were still partly online) As someone with ADHD, I have found that the quiet up there can help me focus because even with headphones, other areas where others are regularly walking by and talking are super distracting to me.
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor

text: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor: The 6th floor of the library has always been a bit of a desolate place, but that's what makes it so good for studying and making sure you're in the right atmosphere for focusing.
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor

text: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor: 6th floor was the MVP for me. Need to study and focus? It's there. Need to take a nap for a few minutes? Boom.
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor

text: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor: The 6th floor is nice, the skylights provide it with lots of natural light.
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor

text: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor: 6th floor is the move
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 6th Floor

text: Student Services Building (SSB / Building 121): Student Services building in the center courtyard where you can see the open sky! It’s usually fairly quiet and stays cool in there. The only drawbacks are lack of outlets and the tables are meant more for eating. Even so, it’s a very peaceful spot. Great spot for 2-3 hours of studying imo.
subject: Student Services Building (SSB / Building 121)

text: Student Services Building (SSB / Building 121): I second this!
subject: Student Services Building (SSB / Building 121)

text: Student Services Building (SSB / Building 121): I think the SSB (spaceship/silver Pringle) has the best quiet spaces since not many people go there
subject: Student Services Building (SSB / Building 121)

text: The Cave (Building 97): The Cave iykyk
subject: The Cave (Building 97)

text: The Cave (Building 97): The Cave (building 97) is a nickname given to the hidden computer lab located across from the cultural center and underneath the marketplace (where Panda Express is). They’re open 9am-5pm, closed on weekends.
subject: The Cave (Building 97)

text: The University Library (Building 15) - 5th Floor: Quiet floor of the library (5th floor). I can focus best there.
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 5th Floor

text: Bronco Student Center (BSC) Games Room: If you like a vibrant atmosphere and noise pollution (I know I do. I can't study in silence) then the game room in the BSC. Not too many places to sit but if you find one it's a cool place.
subject: Bronco Student Center (BSC) Games Room

text: Outdoor Area Behind Building 1: Right behind building 1, it’s outside with a few tables and shade looking at all the greenery (no power though)
subject: Outdoor Area Behind Building 1

text: The Campus Quad: The chairs in the quad
subject: The Campus Quad

text: The University Library (Building 15) - 2nd Floor: 2nd floor campus library whiteboards.
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 2nd Floor

text: Building 9 (College of Engineering): Or vacant rooms in building 9
subject: Building 9 (College of Engineering)

text: The University Library (Building 15) - 4th Floor: 4th floor of the library but the areas with the cushioned chairs. It's not silent, but it's not usually crazy loud like the 3rd floor, it's just right
subject: The University Library (Building 15) - 4th Floor

text: The Koi Pond: Koi pond is great but you might not find many places to sit aside from the grass which may be wet
subject: The Koi Pond

text: Professor Fuh Sang: Professor Fuh Sang is widely recognized by computer science students as one of the best faculty members at Cal Poly Pomona. Her course sections are highly sought after due to her exceptional reputation and consistent teaching quality across the department.
subject: Professor Fuh Sang

text: Professor Fuh Sang: In highly theoretical courses like CS 3110 Formal Languages and Automata, Professor Fuh Sang is described as an incredible instructor. She possesses a deep, mastery-level understanding of the curriculum and excels at breaking complex topics down clearly.
subject: Professor Fuh Sang

text: Professor Fuh Sang: Professor Fuh Sang structures her lectures around heavy practical examples rather than pure theory. Because the curriculum functions like complex logic puzzles, students note that a flexible mind is required, forming study groups is vital, and the course acts as a filter.
subject: Professor Fuh Sang

text: Professor Fuh Sang: Professor Fuh Sang is highly accessible, provides recorded lectures online, and delivers exceptional study notes. Her course tracking includes weekly coding practice, weekly programming projects, two-attempt weekly checkpoint quizzes, a midterm exam, and a final exam.
subject: Professor Fuh Sang

text: Professor Fuh Sang: Professor Fuh Sang is highly receptive to student feedback regarding test formatting and provides all necessary resources to master difficult curriculum. She records her classroom lectures and uploads them to the learning management system for streamlined exam preparation.
subject: Professor Fuh Sang

text: Professor Fuh Sang: Professor Fuh Sang runs a highly organized course schedule with exceptionally clear homework prompts. She explains complex material thoroughly to ensure student comprehension, providing highly granular, detailed feedback on all submitted assignments and examinations.
subject: Professor Fuh Sang

text: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi establishes a fair academic environment in her courses. Student feedback indicates that her examinations, homework assignments, and programming projects align accurately with the course expectations and grading criteria.
subject: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi

text: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi: Students describe Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi as kind, understanding, and straightforward when presenting course concepts. While her heavy reliance on PowerPoint slides can make lectures dry, her direct teaching style ensures the material remains accessible.
subject: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi

text: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi assigns one programming project and one homework assignment every week alongside two midterms and a final. She utilizes an online digital textbook with mandatory interactive exercises, which costs approximately 77 dollars.
subject: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi

text: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi: While Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi aims to teach at a pace suitable for beginners, students note she moves through coding content rapidly. She maintains rigid, exact explanations with unwavering expectations, making her a strong choice for students seeking a challenge.
subject: Professor Tannaz Rezaei Damavandi

text: Professor Fang Tang: Professor Tang is an awesome teacher that will get rid of any bad impressions you've had about CS. I recommend this teacher over any other professor. She is a great instructor and all of her notes are well organized and easy to read. Her exams never have anything tricky or out of the ordinary
subject: Professor Fang Tang

text: Professor Fang Tang: tricky or out of the ordinary She never throws a curved ball at you.
subject: Professor Fang Tang

text: Professor Fang Tang: I really enjoy Dr Tang's classes. Her lectures are very well organized and to the point. Her tests are well thought out and are based on information given in the lectures.
subject: Professor Fang Tang

text: Professor Fang Tang: She's a decent instructor. Her lectures are well organized, and you can use her notes to study without purchasing the textbook. She gives ample examples and exercises during class period. There are a couple quizzes throughout the quarter based on these exercises
subject: Professor Fang Tang

text: Professor Fang Tang: arter based on these exercises Her exams are very straightforward and easy. As a matter of fact, I find it hard to not get an A in her CS241 class. She also allows students to bring one cheat sheet on the midterm and the final
subject: Professor Fang Tang

text: Professor Fang Tang: Her lectures are all on powerpoint/pdf, but are matched very well with the book and she always tries to involve the class with example questions. Tests are straight forward. As long as you can do the homework yourself, you will be able to handle the tests.
subject: Professor Fang Tang

text: Professor Fang Tang: She is a good teacher. At times she fails to communicate things correctly, and sometimes gets confused by what you mean, but these occasions are rare. It isn't hard to pass her class, and you will learn a lot from the projects.
subject: Professor Fang Tang

text: Professor Fang Tang: Daisy is a pretty good instructor. CS130 handles many different ideas and she explains them all very well. The homework is harder than the tests which is good. A very good instructor.
subject: Professor Fang Tang

text: Professor David Dear: This class was online. grade consisted of Midterm, Final, and 3 assignments. Assignments worth 25 points each, the exams were 100 points. The assignments are pretty difficult, but maybe with a tutor, you can get 100% on it
subject: Professor David Dear

text: Professor David Dear: tutor, you can get 100% on it Even though the assignments were difficult, I learned so much!!! I'm glad i took this class. A few of the questions used on the ungraded practice quizes showed up on the exams. Go over power points and read the book.
subject: Professor David Dear

text: Professor David Dear: This professor is very friendly and super cool! He's laid back and very approachable. But make sure you learn how the entities work because you will need this knowledge for CIS305.
subject: Professor David Dear

text: Professor David Dear: Mr. Dear is a wonderful teacher. He makes a somewhat challenging subject, easy and approachable. Always dismisses class early. While we worked with peers in small groups, in class, there was no assigned/graded group project
subject: Professor David Dear

text: Professor David Dear: assigned/graded group project 1 Event Table, 1 Use Case Diagram w/Use Case Narratives, 1 Domain Model, Collaboration and Sequence Diagrams, 1 Class Diagram. Easy tests and final. One of the few CIS teachers who realizes students want to learn something in class AND get a good grade.
subject: Professor David Dear

text: Professor David Dear: A great professor. This class consisted of 6 projects, 4 exams, and a final. The work load can be intimidating but it is actually pretty straightforward. You'll do a good job as long as it makes sense and it meets all the requirements. The lectures were on powerpoint and are available on his website
subject: Professor David Dear

text: Professor David Dear: d are available on his website He posts the solutions after the project is due. He is a fair grader and has an easy going attitude. Dismisses class early but not sure if that'll happen again after a certain incident. I still definitely recommend his class.
subject: Professor David Dear

text: Professor David Dear: Let me just start by saying that I am a loud, obnoxious, and cocky student. Professor Dear is by far the best CIS professor maybe the best professor in Cal Poly. He makes a boring subject actually interesting and easy to understand
subject: Professor David Dear

text: Professor David Dear: resting and easy to understand He probably knows who this is and I want to say thank you for being my professor for cis 101 and cis 310. subject: Professor David Dear

He goes over the material quite well, providing numerous examples. His curve is mathematically precise; he has a formula to calculate it. He will keep you entertained for sure.

text: Professor David Dear: Just 2 tests, mid-term and a final. All the questions are from lecutre. He already has notes made for the class that you can buy. Each homework he will grade it and give comments to your mistakes.

text: Salam Salloum: I would definitely avoid this prof if possible, but also he is not one of the worst professors I have had. The class is not too much work, but he has a student grader, and I felt like I lost points I should not have
subject: Salam Salloum

text: Salam Salloum: lost points I should not have If you complain about your grade it may get fixed, but you have to ask him to check it. He is a nice guy, but lectures can get boring
subject: Salam Salloum

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: this man is a god. def take him if you can. he is an amazing lecturer. he cares about his students and is super chill. the tests are weighted heavily so study (he gives review questions that are super similar to test questions)
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: per similar to test questions) he is so awesome that I asked him to be my senior project supervisor! he's knowledgeable in many fields of CS
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: Dr. Amamra is very good CS professor embodies a sincere commitment to the success of every student. The promise of attaining an A in the course simply by attentively engaging with lectures and completing homework assignments. Dr
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: eting homework assignments. Dr Amamra actively seeks and implements class feedback to continually refine their teaching methodologies.
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: I took this class over the summer. It was a 10-week course with weekly VODs to watch. The assignments were due every week, and exams were conducted online. Both the assignments and exams were quite easy
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: ents and exams were quite easy The instructor is prompt in responding to emails and is understanding if you have vacation plans during the class. Overall, a great teacher.
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: Dr. Amamra is one of the best computer science professors at the school. I have taken two classes with him CS3650 and CS4600. He is extremely knowledgeable and generous in extending deadlines. He offers extra credit opportunities to help students
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: opportunities to help students Some of the lectures can be hard, but if you ask in class, you'll be fine. Great CS professor.
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: This class is difficult but Dr. Amamral makes it wonderful. He goes through every example and PowerPoint to make you truly understand the content of the course. The tests are fair. He gave extra credits
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: re fair. He gave extra credits He is very helpful during office hours he will make sure you understand how to figure everything out.
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: This professor is super helpful and good CS teacher. I had him for both CS2640 and CS3650, and both times his class was well prepared. Clear explanations and expectations; fair exams and gradings. He preferred teamwork and projects
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: referred teamwork and projects He is a knowledgeable professor who can answer any questions you have about the content.
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Abdelfattah Amamra: He is an excellent professor very knowledgeable in his field. He is kind and understanding. He is flexible in due dates. Always there is a second chance to submit late work. He offers extra credits and makeup. He did not move to a new topic unless he is sure that the students have understood.
subject: Abdelfattah Amamra

text: Gilbert Young: Funny Professor! Its just that all class is straight lecture so by halfway through the semester, most people stop coming! There are a couple homework and projects but they are fairly easy to do (the report is tedious). Midterms and final are also on zoom so thats a huge plus
subject: Gilbert Young

text: Gilbert Young: o on zoom so thats a huge plus Overall, decent class but who really cares about operating systems
subject: Gilbert Young

text: Gilbert Young: honestly his lectures aren't that amazing if you are looking to learn but he does his best to make them fun. just a few programming projects and smaller homework assignments, and 2 exams. just make sure you go over the lecture slides and understand the homework and you'll do fine.
subject: Gilbert Young

text: Gilbert Young: 2 Hw, 2 Labs, Midterm and Final. His lectures weren't the best, mainly just attended to not miss any participation discussion board (not too many). Mainly learned from his slides, which were decent. The Labs were simple to do, homework were based on slides
subject: Gilbert Young

text: Gilbert Young: homework were based on slides His midterm and final are based off of his homework with fill in the blanks from his slides.
subject: Gilbert Young

text: Tannaz Damavandi: The Professor just wants to go through AS MANY AS lectures. She does NOT really care that you understand the lecture, or know how to work on the assignment
subject: Tannaz Damavandi

text: Tannaz Damavandi: how to work on the assignment She never gives you the solution, and she does not even try to explain it, that's why you never understand why you did wrong on the exam or assignment.
subject: Tannaz Damavandi

text: Tannaz Damavandi: You are given a semester long group project, make sure you choose good teammates for that project. Two individual projects, both medium difficulty. Two midterms, make sure to study everything on the slides. Study the midterms for the final, alot of overlap. Two homeworks, both fairly easy
subject: Tannaz Damavandi

text: Tannaz Damavandi: wo homeworks, both fairly easy Midterms are hard. Makes you work hard for your A.
subject: Tannaz Damavandi

text:
Title: General & Departmental Advice
At Cal Poly Pomona, freshmen register for Fall classes during orientation. Choosing the earliest possible date is highly recommended. Later dates mean sections fill up quickly, leaving students stuck with inconvenient 8 AM class times and poorly rated professors.
subject: Unknown

text: For freshman computer science majors at CPP, the primary introductory courses are CS 1300 Discrete Structures and CS 1400 Introduction to Programming. Taking them together in the first semester unlocks CS 2400 Data Structures, which opens up the rest of the curriculum.
subject: Unknown

text: Highly rated computer science professors at Cal Poly Pomona include David Johansson, Yu Sun, Navi, and Eger. Students report learning a lot and having a strong chance at an A. Recommended tools for class scheduling include CPPScheduler and the student CPP CS Discord.
subject: Unknown

text: Computer science students at CPP recommend avoiding these specific professors due to poor ratings: Tony Diaz, Edwin Rodriguez, Dominick Atanasio, Saleem Saleem, Professor Sandler, and Thomas Huang. Class seats reserved for later orientation dates often feature these instructors.
subject: Unknown

text: First generation college students at Cal Poly Pomona can find academic guidance, community, and transition support through the CPP CAMINOS program as well as the Educational Opportunity Program, which provides specialized advisors to help map out major requirements.
subject: Unknown

text: For CS 2400 Data Structures at CPP, students must have a solid foundation in basic Java, including arrays, vectors, and maps. Lacking these fundamentals from prerequisite courses will cause significant struggles, as the advanced material builds directly on those core concepts.
subject: Unknown

text: For CS 2560 C++ Programming at CPP, the course functions primarily as an introduction to C++ syntax and language features rather than complex data structures or algorithms. Students focus on translating their foundational Java knowledge into generic problem-solving projects.
subject: Unknown

text: Computer science majors note that CS 3110 Formal Languages and Automata is widely considered one of the most challenging and difficult upper-division courses in the CPP curriculum, requiring significantly more study time and focus than coding-heavy courses like CS 2560.
subject: Unknown

text: The Cal Poly Pomona CS Undergraduate Seminar operates as a student-led presentation course. Following the first week, an approved student delivers an approximately 40-minute lecture on a computer science topic each session, followed by a 15-minute question-and-answer period.
subject: Unknown

text: In addition to weekly student-led lectures, the CPP CS Undergraduate Seminar requires a comprehensive, long-form technical term paper due at the end of the semester. The paper must focus deeply on the specific computer science topic approved for the student's presentation.
subject: Unknown

text: Prior to Spring 2020, CPP computer science majors could petition to substitute the Undergraduate Seminar with a senior project. The department has since removed this option, making the senior project an elective credit and the seminar course 100 percent mandatory to graduate.
subject: Unknown

text: While faculty choice has a smaller impact on a student-led presentation course, computer science majors at CPP explicitly name professors Thanh Nguyen, Nima Davarpanah, and Salam Salloum as the top three notorious instructors to actively avoid within the department.
subject: Unknown

text: Innovation Brew Works: Innovation Brew Works which is campus run has great pizza, beer and root beer floats.
subject: Innovation Brew Works

text: Koji Ramen: Koji Ramen down the street is a local favorite and delicious.
subject: Koji Ramen

text: Claremont Packing House: I would definitely check out the packing house. I used to work at the Whisper House down there and they’ve got a really close really cute down town. 532 W 1st St, Claremont, CA 91711
subject: Claremont Packing House

text: Claremont Packing House: Someone already mentioned it but the Claremont packing house has iron and Kin which a more traditional coffee shop, although fair warning it’s extremely popular and gets crowded(like lines well out the door).
subject: Claremont Packing House

text: The Hat: For late night snacking I would go to the Hat where they have Pastrami fries (good for late night studying fuel)
subject: The Hat

text: Haven City Market: I would also recommend checking out the San Dimas/ West Covina area there a really good brunch place called the broken yolk and they also have a place called haven city market.
subject: Haven City Market

text: Sugar Rush Cafe: My friends and I always enjoyed Sugar Rush Cafe! It's a little hole in the wall breakfast/lunch spot on Valley Blvd. They have great coffee and even better smoothies.
subject: Sugar Rush Cafe

text: Sugar Rush Cafe: It’s surprisingly nice for a place so unassuming
subject: Sugar Rush Cafe

text: Pho 909: Pho 909. Best pho ever, good banh mi and vermicelli and egg rolls too. Nobibi boba shop also right next door.
subject: Pho 909

text: Yupduk (Diamond Bar): Yupduk in diamond bar!!! There’s also a good affordable kbbq place right next to it
subject: Yupduk (Diamond Bar)

text: Mi Cafecito: Mi cafecito in downtown Pomona! It’s like 10-15 minutes away and it’s Mexican inspired coffee? Idk if that’s the right way of putting it but I’d go for yourself.
subject: Mi Cafecito

text: Mi Cafecito: if you like coffee shops: Nambah Cafe in La Verne, Mi Cafecito in Downtown Pomona, Grid Coffee in diamond bar. Thai food from baby elephant. Breakfast bagels and pastries from Bageloo.
subject: Mi Cafecito

text: Grid Coffee: The grid is another coffee place about the same time away in diamond bar, but what makes it good is the matcha which i recommend.

text: Grid Coffee: if you like coffee shops: Nambah Cafe in La Verne, Mi Cafecito in Downtown Pomona, Grid Coffee in diamond bar. Thai food from baby elephant. Breakfast bagels and pastries from Bageloo.

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: For CS 5800 with Nima Davarpanah, you will not learn much, but the grading is great so you cannot complain.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: In graduate-level CS 5800, Professor Davarpanah does not enjoy teaching and the information is very basic. His notes are terrible and read more like hieroglyphics than English. Most of his lecture examples are just generated straight from ChatGPT.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: Professor Davarpanah is by far the worst professor I have ever had. Please do not take this professor for CS 365. It is completely unclear how he still works at Cal Poly Pomona.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: For CS 365, Professor Davarpanah is impossible to reach outside of class. He assigns reading and then fails to follow it in lecture, and the lectures are unrelated to the assignments. Students have to teach themselves the material, though he does hold office hours.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: For CS 2600, Professor Davarpanah is very busy with his outside job and lacks passion for his students. He is impossible to reach under any circumstances. You will have to learn everything yourself through YouTube and LinkedIn. Do not take him if you do not want to waste your time.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: In CS 2600 with Nima Davarpanah, assignments are not graded until the very end of the semester, so you will not know your performance standing. Expect to teach yourself everything via YouTube. Avoid this class unless you want an incredibly stressful experience playing games with your grade.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: For CS 2600, Professor Davarpanah barely grades anything as the semester progresses, leaving students completely in the dark about their actual grades. While the workload is light for the first few weeks, the other critical reviews about his teaching style are completely accurate.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: Professor Davarpanah ghosted our CS 2600 class for most of the semester, leaving us with some recorded videos and basic LinkedIn Learning pathways. He does not answer emails on time and is unreachable during emergencies. You will learn far more from LinkedIn Learning than from him.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: In CS 4650, we were supposed to meet weekly but Professor Davarpanah only showed up four times. His recorded lectures are messy and hard to read. While he is decent one-on-one, it is clear teaching is not his priority. He is not an easy A and you will learn very little.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: During Spring 2022 for CS 2600, Professor Davarpanah only held live class twice and relied on old lectures. He works full-time at a startup company, which makes him completely unreachable via email. He does not have time to lecture or grade the majority of homework or tests.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: Do not take CS 2600 if you want to learn. His lectures are unorganized, his handwritten notes are confusing, and the structure is messy. He only gives a couple of lectures on C, but expects real-world group projects. He is unreachable unless you message him directly on Teams.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: Stay far away from Professor Davarpanah for CS 2600. The course structure is unorganized and lectures are boring. He made us purchase a 100 dollar Raspberry Pi at the start of the term and we barely used it. Expect entirely self-guided LinkedIn Learning modules instead of live classes.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: Avoid CS 2600 with this professor. It was supposed to be a synchronous class, but he dipped after giving only four lectures. He did not grade anything between midterms and the last day before grades were due, skipping a midterm entirely. Everything is completely do-it-yourself.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: In CS 4650, he primarily recycled video recordings from previous semesters. Most of the assigned reading content came from outdated websites and LinkedIn Learning pathways. It takes him 2 to 3 days to reply on Slack or email, showing little effort to actively teach.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: For CS 210, expect fast-paced lectures and difficult exams. Understanding basic concepts guarantees a C on exams and a B on quizzes. Homework is easy, and writing a journal provides simple extra credit. My 72 percent raw total curved to a B, but I still do not recommend.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: For CS 365, you must be ready to study hard independently if you want to master the material. Professor Davarpanah ensures everyone understands concepts before moving on. He is highly helpful during office hours and lectures. I also took him for CS 210 and CS 264.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: This CS 264 class is not for the faint of heart; it feels designed to quickly weed out struggling students, and he will fail you if you do not perform. That said, Nima is an excellent teacher who clearly understands his subject area. Just do not slack off.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Nima Davarpanah: He is a great professor for CS 210. He makes sure the entire class understands the material before moving forward. The course is not the easiest, but it is not the hardest either. I would happily take his classes again for CS 264 and CS 365.
subject: Professor Nima Davarpanah

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: Students widely advise avoiding Professor Thanh Nguyen for computer science courses unless you are a strong self-learner. Reports indicate he moves through complex programming topics incredibly fast during lectures without ensuring the class can keep up with the material.
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: Professor Thanh Nguyen's courses are known for a heavy, stressful workload featuring day-one pop quizzes, weekly quizzes, and weekly programming assignments. While his vague project instructions often cause confusion, the high workload forces students to study consistently.
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: He answers questions quite rudely, someone asked him why they had to code a certain way and he could only answer with "because I said so". Also for a coding teacher he couldn't figure out why his code would break in the middle of lecture
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: break in the middle of lecture I got 10 points (out of 100) off a project for not putting enough spaces.
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: This was a difficult class for an introductory programming course. His lectures were essentially useless because he would go over one topic quickly and expect you to already know it. The quizzes would come right after he taught something new, leaving no time to practice what he had just taught
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: actice what he had just taught Quizzes are given weekly. Two midterms and a final.
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: Tests solely based off content from slides, so you don't have to pay attention to lectures if you review the slides before each quiz. Quizzes once a week instead of after each chapter (why). Ignores comments/emails and gives no feedback. Quiz grade depends on his mood that day
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: e depends on his mood that day Sometimes fair and sometimes taking points off because he can.
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: He was a very tough grader with quizzes every Thursday. He expects you to already know some Java despite it being a beginner class. He is very passive aggressive especially when you ask questions
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Thanh Nguyen: ecially when you ask questions He expects it to be a one and done class essentially going over topics once and expecting the students to have it memorized and learned on the same day.
subject: Professor Thanh Nguyen

text: Professor Qichao Dong: Professor Qichao Dong is highly recommended for CS 2400, with students noting his course features straightforward projects, an easy final exam, and an accessible grading scale. However, he rarely accepts students off the waitlist due to strictly limited class capacities.
subject: Professor Qichao Dong

text: Professor Qichao Dong: Classwork was fragmented across multiple platforms. Mainly used Microsoft Teams, grades were not updated on Canvas. Told students to message him on Teams for assignment grades. Was willing to answer questions, but mainly uses ChatGPT to respond
subject: Professor Qichao Dong

text: Professor Qichao Dong: mainly uses ChatGPT to respond Willing to answer questions, but without AI, would be vague and super broad.
subject: Professor Qichao Dong

text: Professor Qichao Dong: Genuinely cares about his students, but he CANNOT TEACH. He talks slowly and it's very hard to understand him. Getting an A in his class is easy, because he gives a lot of extra credit and genuinely wants you to get an A
subject: Professor Qichao Dong

text: Professor Qichao Dong: enuinely wants you to get an A But if you're looking to actually understand and engage in the material in depth he's not for you.
subject: Professor Qichao Dong

text: Professor Qichao Dong: While the professor is personable, the course structure is hard to navigate. Grades rely heavily on group projects and exams, with no practice problems or study guides provided. Canvas grades were rarely updated, making it hard to track progress. A strong self-study mindset is a must
subject: Professor Qichao Dong

text: Professor Qichao Dong: g self-study mindset is a must I would not retake him.
subject: Professor Qichao Dong

text: Professor Qichao Dong: This instructor does not seem to teach effectively. He insists on using Microsoft Teams instead of Canvas, apparently to be different, but he rarely posts useful information or any study guides on either platform. This makes it difficult for students to follow the course and prepare for exams.
subject: Professor Qichao Dong

text: Professor Peter Laszlo: Professor Peter Laszlo was considered one of the most brilliant and theoretically knowledgeable computer science professors at CPP. While his courses were intensely difficult and not for the faint of heart, students note that the deep conceptual insight gained was well worth the effort.
subject: Professor Peter Laszlo

text: Professor Peter Laszlo: For CS 311, Professor Laszlo held exceptionally high expectations. His lectures were abstract, fast-paced, and required significant self-study to fully grasp. He challenged students with complicated theoretical problems and ran a difficult testing environment with strict grading.
subject: Professor Peter Laszlo

text: Professor Peter Laszlo: In upper-division theory classes, Professor Laszlo administered frequent, highly difficult quizzes straight from the textbook. While average scores frequently sat around 25 percent, he applied a massive final grade curve ensuring that the vast majority of working students passed.
subject: Professor Peter Laszlo

text: Professor Peter Laszlo: For CS 408, Professor Laszlo required students to master Standard ML thoroughly, as it was the core programming language for the course. Key assignments included the highly complex 8-Queens problem. The final grade weight was 38 percent quizzes, 27 percent midterm, and 35 percent final.
subject: Professor Peter Laszlo

text: Professor Peter Laszlo: While Professor Laszlo lacked formal homework, he relied on suggested readings and complex, custom-made logic problems regarding combinations and counting. His testing layout focused entirely on raw conceptual understanding, and he actively curved failing student grades up to a C.
subject: Professor Peter Laszlo

text: Professor Peter Laszlo: For foundational theory courses like CS 1300 and CS 301, Professor Laszlo expected heavy independent reading. Students who understood advanced topics like the Taylor Series performed well. He did not assign standard busywork projects, focusing instead on hardcore theoretical learning.
subject: Professor Peter Laszlo
